did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Convolutional Networks (DCN), specifically modified ResNet50, InceptionV3, and DenseNet121, to perform skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dataset of dermoscopic images. The study evaluated the performance of these deep learning models for melanoma detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, specifically evaluating modified Deep Convolutional Networks (ResNet50, InceptionV3, and DenseNet121) on the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer classification. It includes specific methodology sections (Dataset Preprocessing, Model descriptions), results with performance metrics (accuracy, ROC, AUC), and a comparative analysis of model performance, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Recognition of Skin Cancer from Dermoscopic Images via DNN
journal: 2024 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS 2024)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automatic melanoma detection from dermoscopic images by evaluating the performance of modified Deep Convolutional Networks (DCN), specifically ResNet50, InceptionV3, and DenseNet121. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 images across seven diagnostic categories, and addressed class imbalance through data augmentation techniques. The models were trained and validated using a 70/10/20 split for training, validation, and testing sets, respectively. Performance was assessed using accuracy, Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC), and Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics. The results demonstrated that the modified InceptionV3 model achieved the highest performance, with a weighted accuracy of 86.58% and a weighted ROC of 0.96. The study concludes that DCNs provide a reliable framework for automating melanoma diagnosis and enhancing early skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin cancer (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into seven specific skin cancer/lesion classes using the HAM10000 dataset. The models (ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet121) predict discrete diagnostic labels. Performance is measured using accuracy (ACC), ROC, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly accessible dermatoscopic dataset sourced from https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/DBW86T, containing 10,015 images across seven generic classes of pigmented skin lesions. It is described as a widely recognized resource in the academic community for machine learning research in dermatology.
classes_used: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma / Bowen’s disease (AKIEC)

per_class.NV: Most represented class (outnumbering DF by >58 times)

use.training: 70% of total samples allocated to training dataset; data augmentation techniques were selectively applied to this set to mitigate class imbalance.
use.validation: 10% of total samples reserved for validation dataset; remained unaltered.
use.testing: 20% of total samples designated for testing dataset; remained unaltered.
use.other: Used as a benchmark dataset to evaluate the performance of modified Deep Convolutional Networks (ResNet50, InceptionV3, and DenseNet121) for skin cancer classification.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the performance of modified Deep Convolutional Networks (ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet121) against their unmodified forms and against each other.
To evaluate the classification performance of the models, calculated as (TP + TN) / (TP + FP + TN + FN).
To assess the discriminative ability of the models in distinguishing between different pathological images.
To quantify the overall performance of the models based on the ROC curves.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics such as accuracy, ROC, and AUC.

HAM10000.notes: The modified InceptionV3 achieved the highest performance with a weighted accuracy of 86.58% and a weighted ROC of 0.96. The modified ResNet50 achieved a Test ACC of 81.89% with an ROC score of 0.93. The modified DenseNet121 achieved a Test ACC of 81.88% with an ROC score of 0.93. The modified InceptionV3 made 15 accurate predictions in the test group but misclassified one MEL sample as BKL.

architecture_used.main_model: Modified Deep Convolutional Networks (DCN): ResNet50, InceptionV3, and DenseNet121
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; models were trained from scratch or standard implementations on the HAM10000 dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images from HAM10000 dataset)

modifications: Introduction of new convolution block STAGE1 comprising one CONV BLOCK and three ID BLOCKs.
STAGE1 CONV BLOCK equipped with 32 kernels of size 1x1, 32 kernels of size 3x3, and 192 kernels of size 1x1.
Kernel sizes increased in subsequent stages: 3x3 to 5x5 in STAGE2, and 3x3 to 7x7 in STAGE3 and STAGE4.
Implementation of STAGE1 utilizing a kernel of size 5x5.
Use of kernels of size 5x5 and 7x7 to reduce dimensionality.
Substitution in STAGE5 including 448 kernels of size 1x1, 384 kernels of size 3x3, 384 kernels of size 1x3, and 384 kernels of size 3x1.
Addition of a global average pooling layer.
Addition of a bottleneck layer with a convolution kernel of size 1x1 and growth rate of 6.
Introduction of a convolution block with kernel size 3x3 and growth rate 4 placed before the 1x1 kernel block.
Block layers configuration altered to [4, 4, 8, 8] (from original [2, 2, 2, 2]).

optimizer.ResNet50: Adam (initial learning rate 0.01, reduced by factor of 3 per epoch via ReduceLROnPlateau)

training_details.training_schedule: 70% training, 10% validation, 20% testing split of HAM10000 dataset. Data augmentation applied to training set.

classification_or_output_head.details: Classifies into seven diagnostic categories: AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation applied selectively to the training dataset to mitigate class imbalance
Data augmentation focused on underrepresented classes (VASC and DF) to address disproportionate representation

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Selective Data Augmentation for Class Balancing
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Data augmentation techniques were selectively applied to the training dataset to mitigate class imbalance, focusing more on underrepresented classes (VASC with 142 samples and DF with 115 samples) than on oversampled ones. These operations were exclusively applied to the training sets, while validation and test sets remained unaltered.

conclusions: Modified Deep Convolutional Networks (DCNs), specifically ResNet50, InceptionV3, and DenseNet121, provide a reliable framework for automating melanoma diagnosis and enhancing early skin cancer detection.
The modified InceptionV3 model achieved the highest performance among the tested architectures, with a weighted accuracy of 86.58% and a weighted ROC of 0.96.
The superior performance of the modified InceptionV3 is attributed to its efficient feature extraction capability and acceptable inference time per epoch.
Architectural modifications to the base models significantly enhanced their test accuracy compared to their unmodified forms.
The modified InceptionV3 demonstrated a strong ability to distinguish between different pathological images, with an ROC score of 0.96.
The study identified a limitation in the modified InceptionV3 where one Melanoma (MEL) sample was misclassified as Benign keratosis-like lesions (BKL).
The modified ResNet50 achieved a test accuracy of 81.89% with an ROC score of 0.93.
The modified DenseNet121 achieved a test accuracy of 81.88% with an ROC score of 0.93.
The choice of optimizer (Adam vs. RMSprop) had a negligible effect on test accuracy for the modified models, whereas architectural modifications played a pivotal role in performance enhancement.
The modified InceptionV3 outperformed the other models in terms of both accuracy and reasonable inference time per epoch.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., Melanocytic nevi outnumber Dermatofibroma by >58 times), potentially biasing model performance.
Data augmentation was applied selectively to the training set to mitigate class imbalance, but the validation and test sets remained unaltered, preserving the original distribution for evaluation.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons are based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, ROC, AUC).
The modified InceptionV3 model misclassified one Melanoma (MEL) sample as Benign keratosis-like lesions (BKL), highlighting a critical clinical risk of false negatives for melanoma.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, limiting the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.
No information is provided regarding data leakage risks, patient demographic diversity, or the clinical deployment feasibility of the proposed models.
The paper does not report the loss function used for training, only the optimizers (Adam, RMSprop) and learning rates.
